children.name: Guidelines on Sodium Intake and Noncommunicable Diseases
Background on Noncommunicable Diseases (NCDs)
children.content:
Noncommunicable diseases (NCDs) are the main contributor to mortality and morbidity globally, and interventions to reduce the burden of NCDs are highly cost-effective. Elevated sodium intake has been associated with a number of NCDs (including hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and stroke), and decreasing sodium intake may reduce blood pressure and the risk of associated NCDs. Recent data on sodium intake show that populations around the world are consuming much more sodium than is physiologically necessary. In many cases, they are consuming much more than the current World Health Organization (WHO) recommendation on sodium consumption for adults, which is 2 g sodium/day (equivalent to 5 g salt/day). Since the publication of the previous WHO guideline on sodium intake, an appreciable amount of scientific evidence concerning sodium intake, hypertension, and risk of cardiovascular disease has been published. Member States and international partners therefore requested WHO to review the current guideline on sodium intake for adults and to also generate a guideline on sodium intake for children.

NCDs are the leading cause of death globally, killing more people each year than all other causes combined. The major NCDs currently account for approximately 60% of all deaths and 43% of disease burden globally, and these levels are expected to continue to rise. In 2008, 29 million NCD-related deaths (almost 80%) occurred in low and middle-income countries. In those countries, 29% of NCD-related deaths were in people under 60 years of age; in contrast, in high-income countries, only 13% of such deaths were premature. Hypertension is considered a major risk for cardiovascular disease, especially heart attack and stroke. Suboptimal systolic blood pressure (>115 mmHg) is estimated to contribute to 49% of all coronary heart disease and 62% of all stroke.

children.name: Objective of the Guidelines
children.content: The objective of this guideline is to provide recommendations on the consumption of sodium to reduce NCDs in most adults and children. The recommendations in this guideline can be used by those developing programmes and policies to assess current sodium intake levels relative to a benchmark. If necessary, the recommendations can also be used to develop measures to decrease sodium intake through public health interventions such as food and product labelling, consumer education, and the establishment of food-based dietary guidelines. WHO developed the present evidence-informed guideline using the procedures outlined in the WHO Handbook for guideline development. The steps in this process included: identification of priority questions and outcomes; retrieval of the evidence; assessment and synthesis of the evidence; formulation of recommendations; identification of research gaps; planning for dissemination, implementation, impact evaluation, and updating of the guideline.

children.name: Health Outcomes Related to Sodium
children.content: Sodium is the principal cation in extracellular fluid in the body, and is an essential nutrient necessary for maintenance of plasma volume, acid–base balance, transmission of nerve impulses, and normal cell function. In healthy individuals, nearly 100% of ingested sodium is absorbed during digestion, and urinary excretion is the primary mechanism for maintaining sodium balance. Increased sodium consumption is associated with increased blood pressure, whereas lower sodium consumption appears to decrease blood pressure in adults. A number of recent high-quality systematic reviews of randomized-controlled trials (RCTs) have concluded that decreased sodium intake relative to usual or higher intake results in lowered blood pressure in adults with or without hypertension.

consumption is associated with increased blood pressure, whereas lower sodium consumption appears to decrease blood pressure in adults. A number of recent high-quality systematic reviews of randomized-controlled trials (RCTs) have concluded that decreased sodium intake relative to usual or higher intake results in lowered blood pressure in adults with or without hypertension.

children.name: Potential Adverse Effects of Reduced Sodium
children.content: Some researchers have reported that reduced sodium could potentially have adverse effects on blood lipid concentrations (i.e., increase total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein [LDL] cholesterol and triglyceride, and decrease high-density lipoprotein [HDL] cholesterol), catecholamine levels, or renal function. However, the results showed little or no adverse effect of reduced sodium on blood lipid concentrations. The meta-analysis of studies reporting catecholamine levels showed that reduced sodium intake resulted in a nonsignificant increase in plasma noradrenaline and adrenaline with decreased sodium. Additionally, the results suggest that reduced sodium does not have an adverse effect on renal function and may potentially have a beneficial effect.

children.name: Recommendations for Sodium Intake
children.content: WHO recommends a reduction in sodium intake to reduce blood pressure and risk of cardiovascular disease, stroke, and coronary heart disease in adults. WHO recommends a reduction to <2 g/day sodium (5 g/day salt) in adults. WHO recommends a reduction in sodium intake to control blood pressure in children. The recommended maximum level of intake of 2 g/day sodium in adults should be adjusted downward based on the energy requirements of children relative to those of adults. These recommendations apply to all individuals, with or without hypertension (including pregnant and lactating women), except for individuals with illnesses or taking drug therapy that may lead to hyponatraemia or acute build-up of body water, or require physician-supervised diets.

children.name: Sodium Content in Foods
children.content: The sodium content in various food groups varies significantly. For instance, table salt, baking soda, and baking powder contain approximately 38,000 mg of sodium per 100 g, while processed foods such as bacon and snack foods can contain around 1,500 mg per 100 g. Fresh fruits and vegetables, on the other hand, have much lower sodium content, with fresh fruits containing about 5 mg and fresh vegetables around 10 mg per 100 g. This information can be used for approximate comparisons of food groups and to guide dietary choices.

children.name: Dietary Guidelines for the Brazilian Population
Overview of Dietary Guidelines
children.content:
The Dietary Guidelines for the Brazilian population present a set of information and recommendations about food aimed at promoting the health of individuals, families, communities, and Brazilian society as a whole, both now and in the future. This guide is intended for all Brazilians, including workers whose profession involves promoting the health of the population, such as health professionals, community agents, educators, and human resources trainers. These workers will be fundamental for the widespread dissemination of this material and for ensuring that the content is understood by all, including those with reading difficulties. It is hoped that this guide will be used in people's homes, health units, schools, and any space where health promotion activities take place, such as community centers, social assistance reference centers, unions, training centers for workers, and social movement headquarters. Although the focus of this material is on health promotion and disease prevention, its recommendations may also be useful for those suffering from specific diseases. In such cases, it is essential that nutritionists adapt the recommendations to the specific conditions of each individual, supporting health professionals in organizing nutritional care.

Every structured human action is implicitly or explicitly guided by principles. The formulation of dietary guidelines is no exception. The principles that guided the preparation of this guide are presented in this chapter. Food is more than nutrient intake; it concerns the foods that contain and provide nutrients, how foods are combined and prepared, the characteristics of eating habits, and the cultural and social dimensions of food practices. All these aspects influence health and well-being. Nutrient intake, provided by food, is essential for good health. Equally important for health are the specific foods that provide nutrients, the numerous possible combinations between them, and their preparation methods, as well as the characteristics of eating habits and the cultural and social dimensions of food practices. The science of nutrition emerged with the identification and isolation of nutrients present in foods and with studies on the effect of individual nutrients on the incidence of certain diseases. These studies were fundamental for formulating policies and actions aimed at preventing specific nutritional deficiencies and cardiovascular diseases associated with excessive sodium or animal fat consumption. However, the effect of individual nutrients has progressively proven insufficient to explain the relationship between food and health.

children.name: General Recommendations
children.content: This chapter presents general recommendations that guide the choice of foods to compose a nutritionally balanced, tasty, culturally appropriate diet that also promotes socially and environmentally sustainable food systems. These recommendations were developed according to the principles outlined in the previous chapter and aim to maximize the health and well-being of all, now and in the future. The recommendations emphasize the type of processing that foods undergo before acquisition, preparation, and consumption. The type of processing employed in their production conditions the nutrient profile, taste, and flavor they add to the diet, as well as influencing with which other foods they will be consumed, in what circumstances (when, where, with whom), and even in what quantity. Four categories of foods, defined according to the type of processing employed in their production, are covered by the recommendations of this chapter.

Four categories of foods, defined according to the type of processing employed in their production, are covered by the recommendations of this chapter.

children.name: Food Categories
children.content: The first category includes in natura or minimally processed foods. In natura foods are those obtained directly from plants or animals and acquired for consumption without any alteration after leaving nature. Minimally processed foods are in natura foods that have undergone minimal alterations before acquisition. Examples include dried grains, polished and packaged or ground into flour, washed roots and tubers, refrigerated or frozen cuts of meat, and pasteurized milk. The second category corresponds to products extracted from in natura foods or directly from nature and used by people to season and cook foods and create culinary preparations. Examples of these products include oils, fats, sugar, and salt. The third category corresponds to products manufactured essentially with the addition of salt or sugar to an in natura or minimally processed food, such as pickled vegetables, canned fruits, cheeses, and breads. The fourth category corresponds to products whose manufacturing involves various stages and techniques of processing and multiple ingredients, many of which are used exclusively in industrial settings. Examples include soft drinks, filled cookies, packaged snacks, and instant noodles.

children.name: Practical Eating Steps
children.content:
Make in natura or minimally processed foods the basis of your diet. In natura or minimally processed foods, in a wide variety and predominantly of plant origin, are the foundation for a nutritionally balanced, tasty, culturally appropriate diet that promotes a socially and environmentally sustainable food system. These foods include many varieties of grains, tubers and roots, legumes and vegetables, fruits, milk, eggs, fish, meats, and also water. The acquisition of in natura foods is limited to some varieties such as fruits, vegetables, greens, roots, tubers, and eggs. Even so, it is common for even these foods to undergo some alteration before being acquired, such as cleaning, removal of inedible parts, and refrigeration. Other foods like rice, beans, milk, and meat are commonly acquired after drying, packaging, pasteurization, cooling, or freezing. The guide recommends making in natura or minimally processed foods the basis of your diet due to their nutritional benefits and their role in promoting a sustainable food system.

1. Make in natura or minimally processed foods the basis of your diet. 2. Use oils, fats, salt, and sugar in small quantities when seasoning and cooking foods and creating culinary preparations. 3. Limit the consumption of processed foods, consuming them in small quantities as ingredients in culinary preparations or as part of meals based on in natura or minimally processed foods. 4. Avoid ultraprocessed foods. 5. Eat regularly and attentively, in appropriate environments, and whenever possible, in company. 6. Shop in places that offer a variety of in natura or minimally processed foods. 7. Develop, practice, and share culinary skills. 8. Plan the use of time to give food the space it deserves. 9. Prefer, when eating out, places that serve freshly prepared meals. 10. Be critical of information, guidance, and messages about food conveyed in commercial advertisements.

children.name: Identifying Obstacles
children.content: This chapter addresses potential obstacles to adopting the recommendations regarding food choices, food combinations, and the act of eating. These obstacles are identified as: information, availability, cost, culinary skills, time, and advertising. Overcoming these obstacles may be easier or more difficult depending on the nature of the obstacle, the resources available to individuals, and the environment in which they live. Some obstacles may be completely and quickly removed, while others will require persistence. In some cases, removing obstacles will require individuals to reflect on the importance of food in their lives and to assign greater value to the process of acquiring, preparing, and consuming food. In other cases, it will require public policies and regulatory actions by the state to make the environment more conducive to adopting the recommendations.

children.name: Strategies to Overcome Obstacles
children.content: To overcome the obstacles identified, individuals can take several actions. For information, use this guide as a reliable source and discuss its content with family and friends. For availability, shop at markets and fairs that offer in natura or minimally processed foods. For cost, prefer seasonal fruits and vegetables and consider community-supported agriculture. For culinary skills, develop and share cooking skills with others. For time, plan meals and share responsibilities for food preparation with family members. For advertising, educate children about the purpose of advertising and advocate for regulations that protect consumers from misleading food marketing.

children.name: Dietary Guidance to Improve Cardiovascular Health
Overview of Dietary Guidance
children.content: Poor diet quality is strongly associated with elevated cardiovascular disease morbidity and mortality risk. This American Heart Association scientific statement for food-based cardiovascular health optimization and cardiovascular disease risk reduction guidance summarizes available evidence and provides contextual guidance for the key features of heart-healthy dietary patterns. It enumerates collateral benefits of adopting a heart-healthy dietary pattern in terms of nutrient intake adequacy and compatibility with other chronic disease risk reduction guidance. The features of a heart-healthy dietary pattern include (1) adjusting energy intake and expenditure to achieve and maintain a healthy body weight; (2) eating plenty of vegetables and fruits and choosing a wide variety; (3) choosing foods made mostly with whole grains rather than refined grains; (4) choosing healthy sources of protein; (5) choosing sources of unsaturated fats in place of sources of saturated fat; (6) choosing minimally processed foods instead of ultraprocessed foods; (7) minimizing intake of added sugars in beverages and foods; (8) reducing sodium intake by choosing foods low in sodium and preparing foods with minimal or no salt; and (9) if alcohol is not consumed, do not start; if alcohol is consumed, limit intake.

children.name: Features of Heart-Healthy Dietary Patterns
children.content: The Table summarizes the features of a heart-healthy dietary pattern. The origins of CVD begin early in life. Establishing a heart-healthy dietary pattern is fundamental to maintaining cardiovascular health throughout the life course. The relevance of diet quality on cardiovascular health starts as early as the prenatal period. Prepregnancy maternal dietary patterns and other lifestyle behaviors are associated with risk of gestational diabetes and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. Dietary patterns early in life are linked not only to childhood cardiovascular parameters such as obesity, metabolic syndrome, hypertension, dyslipidemia, and type 2 diabetes but also to lifelong dietary patterns and cardiovascular risk. Thus, a heart-healthy dietary pattern is recommended for children starting at 1 year of age and is a key component of a favorable cardiovascular health trajectory throughout adulthood.

children.name: Vegetables and Fruits
children.content: Whole or minimally processed vegetables and fruits are core components of heart-healthy dietary patterns. These patterns are consistently associated with cardiovascular health, including blood lipids, blood pressure, and type 2 diabetes control. Vegetables and fruits provide essential nutrients and dietary fiber, which supports gastrointestinal function, provides fuel for the gut microbiota, and plays a role in the regulation of blood glucose. Dietary patterns rich in fiber have been associated with reduced risk of CVD, type 2 diabetes, and colorectal cancer. Good sources include vegetables, fruits, whole grains, nuts, legumes, and seeds.

Vegetables and fruits provide essential nutrients and dietary fiber, which supports gastrointestinal function, provides fuel for the gut microbiota, and plays a role in the regulation of blood glucose. Dietary patterns rich in fiber have been associated

plays a role in the regulation of blood glucose. Dietary patterns rich in fiber have been associated with reduced risk of CVD, type 2 diabetes, and colorectal cancer. Good sources include vegetables, fruits, whole grains, nuts, legumes, and seeds.

children.name: Whole Grains
children.content: Whole grains are defined by the presence of all 3 components of the grain kernel—the starchy endosperm, inner germ, and outer bran—and provide a complex matrix of dietary fiber, vitamins, minerals, and other bioactive compounds. Commonly consumed whole grains are whole wheat, oats, brown rice, quinoa, barley, and rye. A substantial body of observational evidence from large cohort studies demonstrates that regular consumption of whole-grain foods compared with infrequent intake is associated with lower risks of CVD, coronary heart disease, stroke, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome and more favorable cardiovascular risk factors, including blood pressure, blood lipids, and glycemic control.

Commonly consumed whole grains are whole wheat, oats, brown rice, quinoa, barley, and rye. A substantial body of observational evidence from large cohort studies demonstrates that regular consumption of whole-grain foods compared with infrequent intake is associated

nstrates that regular consumption of whole-grain foods compared with infrequent intake is associated with lower risks of CVD, coronary heart disease, stroke, type 2 diabetes, and metabolic syndrome and more favorable cardiovascular risk factors, including blood pressure, blood lipids, and glycemic control.

children.name: Healthy Sources of Protein
children.content: Protein is an essential nutrient for growth, development, and overall health, but the relationship between protein quantity and cardiovascular health is uncertain. Recommendations for protein center on promoting heart-healthy protein sources which are determined predominantly by fatty acid profile and fiber content. Legumes (beans, peas, and lentils) and nuts are good sources of protein and are rich in unsaturated fat and fiber. Dietary patterns higher in legumes and lower in red and processed meat have been associated with lower CVD and coronary heart disease risk. Likewise, dietary patterns higher in nuts have been associated with lower CVD and all-cause mortality risk.

Legumes (beans, peas, and lentils) and nuts are good sources of protein and are rich in unsaturated fat and fiber. Dietary patterns higher in legumes and lower in red and processed

rich in unsaturated fat and fiber. Dietary patterns higher in legumes and lower in red and processed meat have been associated with lower CVD and coronary heart disease risk. Likewise, dietary patterns higher in nuts have been associated with lower CVD and all-cause mortality risk.

children.name: Sources of Unsaturated Fat
children.content: Clinical trial evidence consistently shows that replacing sources of saturated fat with sources of polyunsaturated fat and, to a lesser extent, sources of monounsaturated fat reduces low-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentrations, a causal risk factor for CVD. Strong evidence shows that replacing butter with plant oils and spreads containing predominantly unsaturated fat decreases low-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentrations. Limited evidence shows that substituting butter with plant oils and spreads with predominantly unsaturated fat is associated with a lower CVD morbidity and mortality risk.
chunks: Clinical trial evidence consistently shows that replacing sources of saturated fat with sources of polyunsaturated fat and, to a lesser extent, sources of monounsaturated fat reduces low-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentrations, a causal risk factor for CVD. Strong evidence shows that replacing butter with plant oils and spreads containing predominantly unsaturated fat decreases low-density lipoprotein cholesterol concentrations. Limited evidence shows that substituting butter with plant

y lipoprotein cholesterol concentrations. Limited evidence shows that substituting butter with plant oils and spreads with predominantly unsaturated fat is associated with a lower CVD morbidity and mortality risk.

children.name: Minimizing Added Sugars
children.content: Added sugars are defined as all forms of sugar that are added to foods and beverages during processing or preparation. There is strong evidence to support recommendations to minimize added sugar intake across the life course. Dietary patterns high in added sugars are consistently associated with adverse cardiovascular health and higher CVD risk. Systematic reviews have linked sugar-sweetened beverages to higher risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, coronary heart disease, and cardiovascular mortality.

children.name: Low Sodium Foods
children.content: In general, dietary sodium intake and potassium intake have opposite effects on blood pressure, the leading modifiable risk factor for preventable mortality. An increased intake of sodium chloride (salt) raises blood pressure, whereas an increased intake of potassium lowers blood pressure. Reducing sodium intake lowers blood pressure in both nonhypertensive and hypertensive individuals. In prospective cohort studies, lower sodium intakes are associated with blunted age-related rise in systolic blood pressure and lower CVD risk.

children.name: Energy Intake and Expenditure
children.content: Preserving a healthy body weight across the life course is central to optimizing cardiovascular health. Currently, obesity affects 21% of children and adolescents, and 40% of adults in the United States. Excess body fat contributes to the development of type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular-kidney metabolic syndrome, which negatively influences cardiovascular health. Excess energy intake coupled with inadequate physical activity results in positive energy balance and subsequent overweight and obesity. Individual energy needs depend on a number of factors such as age, sex, height, weight, physical activity level, and pregnancy or lactation status. Energy intake should be adjusted to achieve and maintain a healthy body weight. Dietary habits and dietary patterns are shared, experienced, and transmitted among household members. They are learned early in life, although there are key inflection points where dietary trajectories can change along the life course, including childhood, adolescence, and young adulthood.

children.name: Introdução à Saúde Cardiovascular
children.content:
As doenças cardiovasculares estão associadas a pior qualidade de vida e altos níveis de mortalidade. Um padrão alimentar que protege a saúde do coração vai além de consumir alimentos “bons” ou “ruins”. É necessário equilíbrio e variedade na alimentação. Os padrões alimentares cardioprotetores se caracterizam pela inclusão de frutas e vegetais, grãos integrais, fontes saudáveis de proteínas, alimentos minimamente processados, pequenas quantidades de açúcar, sal e gorduras. Portanto, manter hábitos alimentares adequados é muito importante tanto na prevenção quanto no controle da doença cardiovascular já presente. Diante disso, para ajudar na adoção de hábitos alimentares cardioprotetores, este manual foi elaborado pela equipe de nutricionistas da Cardiologia do Hospital de Clínicas de Porto Alegre, a partir do Guia Alimentar para a População Brasileira e da literatura científica mais atual sobre o assunto.

De acordo com a Organização Mundial da Saúde (OMS), cerca de três quartos das mortes por doenças cardiovasculares podem ser prevenidas por mudanças de estilo de vida e controle de fatores de risco, como hipertensão, diabetes, obesidade, dislipidemia etc. O que mais favorece o desenvolvimento de doenças cardiovasculares é obesidade, sedentarismo, tabagismo, consumo excessivo de álcool, diabetes não controlada e dieta não saudável.

favorece o desenvolvimento de doenças cardiovasculares é obesidade, sedentarismo, tabagismo, consumo excessivo de álcool, diabetes não controlada e dieta não saudável.

children.name: Dietas Específicas
children.content: Exemplos de padrões alimentares que protegem a saúde do coração são: a dieta Dash e a dieta mediterrânea. A dieta Dash é um padrão alimentar que foi desenvolvido para pessoas com hipertensão arterial e que demonstra benefícios cardiovasculares, como melhor controle da pressão arterial, dos níveis de glicose no sangue, da fome e da saciedade. Ela também ajuda a reduzir o colesterol ruim (LDL) e aumentar o colesterol bom (HDL). Essa dieta é composta por alimentos ricos em fibras, vitaminas e minerais, como feijões, carnes magras, laticínios com baixo teor de gordura, grãos integrais, frutas e legumes, e possui baixa quantidade de sal, doces e gordura saturada. A Dieta Mediterrânea, por sua vez, é um padrão alimentar voltado para quem precisa de uma alimentação saudável e estimula o consumo de legumes, frutas, verduras, peixe, grãos integrais e azeite de oliva.

e aumentar o colesterol bom (HDL). A Dieta Mediterrânea, por sua vez, é um padrão alimentar voltado para quem precisa de uma alimentação saudável e estimula o consumo de legumes, frutas, verduras, peixe, grãos integrais e azeite de oliva.

children.name: Componentes de um Prato Saudável
children.content: Metade do prato deve ser composto por legumes e vegetais, um quarto por cereais e o outro um quarto por proteínas e leguminosas. Para carboidratos, prefira grãos integrais, batata doce, mandioca e mandioquinha, evitando cereais refinados. Para frutas, prefira as in natura, evitando frutas em calda e sucos adoçados. Nas leguminosas, prefira feijões, lentilha e grão-de-bico, evitando feijoadas com linguiça. Para proteínas, prefira frango sem pele, peixes assados/cozidos, ovos e carne bovina magra, evitando carnes gordurosas e fritas.

Nas leguminosas, prefira feijões, lentilha e grão-de-bico, evitando feijoadas com linguiça. Para proteínas, prefira frango sem pele, peixes assados/cozidos, ovos e carne bovina
s com linguiça. Para proteínas, prefira frango sem pele, peixes assados/cozidos, ovos e carne bovina magra, evitando carnes gordurosas e fritas.

children.name: Processamento de Alimentos
children.content: A base da alimentação deve ser composta por alimentos in natura ou minimamente processados. Alimentos in natura são aqueles que não sofreram qualquer alteração após deixar a natureza ou que sofreram alterações mínimas, como frutas, verduras, legumes, leite, iogurte natural, feijões, cereais, raízes, tubérculos, ovos e carnes resfriadas ou congeladas. Alimentos ultraprocessados, por outro lado, são produtos feitos pela indústria que possuem muitos ingredientes, sendo a maioria deles aditivos químicos, e uma pequena proporção de alimentos in natura. Exemplos incluem biscoitos, sucos em pó, refrigerantes e embutidos, que devem ser consumidos com moderação.

children.name: Consumo de Bebidas
children.content: É importante destacar que não existe uma quantidade segura de consumo de bebidas alcoólicas e esse hábito pode causar danos ao organismo. O consumo de no máximo 30g de álcool por dia para homens e 20g por dia para mulheres, quando saudáveis, parece oferecer menor risco. Exemplos de quantidades de álcool em diferentes bebidas incluem: 90ml de vinho tinto (11g de álcool), 350ml de cerveja (17g de álcool) e 50ml de destilado (20g de álcool).

children.name: Dicas de Planejamento
children.content: As tabelas a seguir oferecem orientações sobre o que preferir, consumir com moderação e evitar em diferentes grupos alimentares. Para cereais, prefira alimentos integrais, consuma moderadamente pães brancos e evite biscoitos e cereais açucarados. Para vegetais, prefira vegetais crus e cozidos, evitando os preparados na manteiga. Para frutas, prefira frescas ou congeladas sem adição de açúcares, evitando geleias com açúcar. Para carnes, prefira peixes e frango sem pele, evitando salsichas e carnes gordurosas. Para laticínios, prefira opções semidesnatadas e desnatadas, evitando nata e creme de leite. Para cozinhar, utilize azeite de oliva e evite manteiga e gorduras trans.

children.name: Receitas Saudáveis
children.content: O manual também inclui receitas de molhos, pastas e temperos substitutos de sal. Por exemplo, um molho pode ser feito com cebola, alho, semente de mostarda, noz-moscada, páprica, cravo, óleo vegetal e vinagre. Outra receita é uma pasta de grão-de-bico que inclui grão-de-bico cozido, alho, azeite e salsinha. Além disso, há uma receita de maionese caseira sem óleo, que utiliza ovos cozidos, iogurte natural, limão e alho, ideal para acompanhar saladas e aperitivos.
